data.title: Biohaven's Exciting Year Of Catalysts
data.description:
Following Biohaven's (NYSE:BHVN) acquisition by Pfizer (PFE), this "new" venture led by Vlad Coric has had a successful and steady increase in valuation rising from an approximate $7 share price and $300M valuation up to the current $~40 and nearly $4B valuation. We believe that despite the large rise in valuation, there is still substantial upside to the story with the potential success of Biohaven's later stage clinical assets that have meaningful catalysts in 2025. Given the current stock price and upcoming catalyst calendar, supported by strong data, we assign a 12-month target price of $60, near the previous highs that the stock has seen.

Technical Analysis

Although this spinout is fairly new, having listed in September 2023, the company has seen consecutive successes and a stock price that shows no signs of slowing down. The recent pullback in shares has tested a level of support in the mid $30’s that was established in the summer of 2024, not dipping below $32. We believe the stock will continue to see support around $35 with great upside potential as the catalyst calendar provides momentum throughout the year.

Pipeline Overview

Biohaven has a robust pipeline touching on fields that include CNS, immunology and oncology. Below are three key assets that we view equally contribute to the majority of the company’s overall valuation due to their data, upcoming milestones and development stage:

  • Troriluzole (BHV-4157)

    • Spinocerebellar Ataxia (SCA)
    • Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)

  • Taldefgrobep alfa (BHV-2000)

    • Spinal Muscular Atrophy
    • Obesity

  • Kv7 Activator (BHV-7000)

    • Focal Epilepsy
    • Generalized Epilepsy
    • Bipolar Disorder
    • Major Depressive Disorder

There are several more clinical candidates spanning several indications including BHV-8000, BHV-1300, BHV-1310, BHV-1400, BHV-1600, BHV-1510, BHV-1530, BHV-1500. However, all of these candidates have yet to progress into Phase 2 development and do not contribute as meaningfully to Biohaven’s overall valuation.

The large diversity among assets that Biohaven has, combined with various stages of development, mitigate most binary event risk when it comes to clinical trial development. This risk mitigation will preserve company valuation in the case of unfavorable data, which is an ongoing concern in the biotech space.

Key Assets

Troriluzole (BHV-4157)

Biohaven’s lead clinical asset, BHV-4157, is an oral glutamate modulator currently in two Phase 3 trials in OCD and SCA, with SCA recently having an NDA accepted by the FDA. Glutamate is an important neurotransmitter present in 90% of all brain synapses and plays an essential role in normal brain functioning. The company believes that abnormal levels of glutamate may lead to neural dysfunction and nerve cell death underlying ataxia, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), and other neurodegenerative disorders.

The NDA for use in SCA comes on the back of positive topline results from its pivotal Phase 3 study which demonstrated that Troriluzole dosed at 200mg daily slowed disease progression of 50-70%, which the company believes positions Troriluzole as the first treatment to delay SCA disease progression, giving patients additional years of functional independence.

The results from the Phase 3 trial are a strong indication that there is a high likelihood of approval and commercialization by year end.

Taldefgrobep Alfa (BHV-2000)

Taldefgrobep alfa is a human recombinant protein designed to bind to myostatin and to function as an activin receptor antagonist, leading to the blocking of myostatin signaling in skeletal muscles. Myostatin acts as a negative regulator of muscle growth, and blocking myostatin activity and signaling has been shown to improve muscle function and strength in disease models of neuromuscular wasting.

Unfortunately, Biohaven reported topline results from its Phase 3 trial of taldefgrobep in November with plans to meet with the FDA to discuss the results, which failed to achieve the primary endpoint of the study. However, there were upsides which demonstrated efficacy signals in clinically relevant and biomarker-defined subgroups. We may see upside on this indication in the future, but its weighing is low.

Positively, Biohaven intends to initiate a Phase 2 trial of taldefgrobep in obesity based on robust myostatin reduction and favorable changes in body composition observed in taldefgrobep-treated subjects. The body composition data observed in the RESILIENT trial in SCA have changed the priority to obesity with accelerated clinical development. Given concerns of GLP-1 therapy and the high density of weight reduction due to lean mass loss, taldefgrobep could be well positioned to become a new standard of care.

Kv7 Activator (BHV-7000)

If you are familiar with any biology, you know the importance and role of the sodium/potassium channels. Kv7 is a protein that forms a channel that regulates the flow of charged potassium ions across cell membranes, repolarizing nerve cells to reset the channel to continue functioning. There are four programs in the Kv7 platform that target focal epilepsy, generalized epilepsy, bipolar disorder, and major depressive disorder. As a whole, BHV-7000 demonstrated superior pharmacological activity when compared to ezogabine, the current leading preclinical model of epilepsy.

In January 2024, Biohaven completed its End-of-Phase 2 meeting with the FDA and subsequently initiated two Phase 2/3 trials of BHV-7000 for the treatment of focal epilepsy in 1H 2024. The pivotal trials aim to evaluate the primary efficacy endpoint which is the change from baseline in 28-day average seizure frequency, while secondary endpoints include 50% responder rate (% of subjects with 50%+ reduction in monthly seizure frequency), 75% responder rate, seizure freedom, and change from baseline in Patient Global Impression of Change (PGI-C), along with safety and tolerability. Both of the Phase 2/3 trials of BHV-7000 in focal epilepsy are expected to complete their respective treatment phases by the end of 3Q 2025 with topline data in 1H 2026.

In addition to focal epilepsy, Biohaven initiated a Phase 2/3 study in generalized epilepsy with the primary efficacy endpoint of the study being time to second day with generalized tonic-clonic seizure, while secondary endpoints include seizure freedom and safety/tolerability. These results are not expected until at least 2H 2026.

Regarding Bipolar disorder, BHV-7000 demonstrated positive effects in modulating impulse behavior through a novel mechanism of action while maintaining a safe and tolerable profile. In May 2024, the company initiated a Phase 2/3 trial that is expected to provide topline results in 1H 2025.

Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)

Biohaven expects BHV-7000 to be differentiated from current SOC antidepressants with its potentially superior safety and tolerability profile, which avoids typical side effects associated with SSRIs/SNRIs. In preclinical studies, BHV-7000 demonstrated similar therapeutic effects when compared to ketamine, a highly potent antidepressant, in the 5-Choice Operant rodent model of MDD. Additionally, the combination of BHV-7000 and an NMDA antagonist lanicemine (BHV-5500) led to significant additive effects as measured using the Progressive Ratio Operant Test in the rodent model of depression, in support of further development of BHV-7000 in MDD.

Biohaven initiated a Phase 2 clinical trial of BHV-7000 in this indication in May 2024, with topline results expected in 2H 2025. The primary endpoint of the study is the Montgomery–Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) from baseline to week 6. Secondary endpoints include CGI-S, Snaith-Hamilton Pleasure Scale (SHAPS).

Key Upcoming Catalysts

As mentioned, 2025 and into 1H 2026 is filled with catalysts which can be seen on the company’s Pipeline page. The company anticipates approximately 11 key catalysts during this time, with around half of them being binary data readouts that can meaningfully impact the company.

1H Catalysts

  • Topline Phase 3 Data in OCD #1 (BHV-2000)
  • Initiate Phase 2 in obesity with Taldefgrobep (BHV-2000)
  • Pivotal study topline data in Bipolar Disorder (BHV-7000)
  • POC topline data in migraine and pain (BHV-2100)
  • Initiate Phase 2/3 in Parkinson’s disease (BHV-8000)
  • Initiate Phase 1 in Myasthenia Gravis (BHV-1310)

2H Catalysts

  • FDA Decision and potential commercialization of Troriluzole (BHV-4157) in SCA
  • Phase 3 study topline data in OCD #2 (BHV-2000)
  • Pivotal study topline data in major depressive disorder (BHV-7000)
  • Initiate Phase 2 in Graves disease (BHV-1300)
  • Phase 1 interim data in epithelial tumors (BHV-1510)

Risks

While the upside is attractive with the number of upcoming catalysts, these binary events and data readouts carry tremendous risk. Starting out the year with negative-(ish) results from the pivotal trial of troriluzole in SCA, it is a hit to confidence.

There is also a platform risk with Kv7. Having based the platform and CNS portfolio around this protein, negative efficacy in one of the late-stage trials could signal carry over into the other indications, potentially spelling an end to the platform.

The largest risk, however, is the financing needs. As of October 2, 2024, the company reported approximately $642M in cash for its operations, which includes the ~$270M from the offering completed in October. While this cash position is larger than the market cap of many small-cap biotech companies, Biohaven burns a lot of money. Approximately $660M through the first 9 months of 2024 to be exact.

There is a large financing overhang for the company. Even with the offering in late 2024, they will likely need to complete an additional financing in the second half of 2025. This can hamper potential gains provided by positive data readouts that are scattered throughout the year.

Conclusion

Biohaven has a deep clinical development pipeline with assets spanning several indications and disease areas. This diversification provides reasonable risk mitigation to investors who are looking to invest in biotech companies that have upside potential without the drawbacks of singular binary data events that smaller companies often face.

With novel therapeutic approaches, strong data sets across its late stage assets, and an experienced leadership team, Biohaven is well positioned to capitalize on the many upcoming catalysts that, we believe, will bring significant shareholder value. We assign a $60 price target through 2025 and despite the cash cliff the company is facing, we believe under $40 Biohaven is a strong buy.

data.title: Transocean: The Inflection Point Has Not Been Reached Just Yet
data.description:
One industry that I have always been very interested by has been the energy industry. It is incredibly diverse, and it is a space that should continue to see growing demand for many years to come. However, different segments of that space have different prospects. One segment that has been pushed down quite a bit over the last several years has been the offshore drilling market. Even recently, shares of some of these companies have not been great. As an example, we need only look at Transocean (NYSE:RIG). Back in October of last year, I reaffirmed the company as a 'hold' candidate. This was based off of continued profit issues, high debt levels, and falling backlog.

Since that time, shares have fallen by 16%. That's far worse than the 3.1% upside achieved by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. The good news is that the most recent data provided by management has been a bit more promising. Under the right circumstances, shares of the business could be reasonably attractive after adjusting for risk. But with backlog still proving to be a problem, I'm not yet sold on the business. Instead, I believe that maintaining it as a 'hold' candidate makes the most sense.

Staying the course

In my last article about Transocean, I was able to cover through the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. But now, results extend through 2024 as a whole. During the most recent quarter, we saw some interesting results. Revenue for the company came in at $952 million. That represents an increase of 28.5% compared to the $741 million reported one year earlier. This rather substantial rise in sales can really be attributed to a couple of factors. For starters, the company saw a rise in its rig utilization rate. Back in the final quarter of 2023, it came in at 51.6%. But fast-forward a single year, and that number had grown to 66.8%.

In addition to this, the average daily revenue generated by vessels in its fleet jumped 6.6% from $407,800 to $434,700. While urging caution, Westwood Global Energy Group, a subscription-based data service that focuses on the energy markets, detailed how 2024 was a time of strength for the industry from a day rate perspective. They pointed out that some floating rig rates were above $500,000 per day. And during the year, there was even one instance of an eighth generation drill ship that focuses on high specification work, landing a rate of over $600,000 per day. Clearly, demand in this market has gotten stronger over the last year or so, and that has shown in the form of additional revenue.

This is obviously a net positive for Transocean and its investors. Because of the higher revenue, the company went from generating a loss of $104 million in the final quarter of 2023 to generating a profit of $7 million. Adjusted net profits went from negative $74 million to positive $27 million. In addition to this, operating cash flow for the company expanded from $98 million to $206 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $22 million to $156 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $122 million to $323 million.

In the chart above, you can see that the final quarter of 2024 was not a one-time thing. 2024 as a whole ended up being better across the board for shareholders. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all got better during this time. A rise in the firm's utilization rate from 51.9% to 60.5%, as well as an increase in any average day rate that it charges from $382,300 to $430,100, was responsible for all of this. In most respects, the company did better than it did the previous year.

One really great thing about Transocean is that it truly is an industry leader. The company owns the highest specification fleet out of any player out there. Furthermore, as the image below illustrates, the company boasts future committed floater utilization rates that are quite a bit above other similar players in the industry. Furthermore, the company seems optimistic about opportunities in the floater space over the next 24 months. They claim that 96 rig years are to be awarded across 78 programs during this window of time. This opens the door for significant revenue generation.

There are even more good things to say about the company at this time. You see, one problem with this space has been that leverage tends to get too high. That creates a lot of risk for the companies in the industry and for their respective shareholders. However, management has been working to reduce this ever since net debt peaked at $6.67 billion in the first quarter of 2023. It has been a pretty choppy ride since then. But as of the end of the most recent quarter, net debt had pulled back somewhat to $5.94 billion. In all likelihood, debt is going to continue dropping. Management claimed that they want to reduce debt this year by $715 million. This will be paired with growth in order to bring the firm's net leverage ratio down to 3.5 by late 2026. To put this in perspective, the business ended 2024 with a net leverage ratio of about 5.2.

Unfortunately, I can't say that everything has been great. For those who watched the company's financial results from quarter to quarter, the third quarter of 2024 may have seemed like a strong time for the company. After all, backlog for the company had surged to $9.29 billion. This was up from the $8.64 billion reported just a single quarter earlier.

However, this was short-lived, with the overall backlog contracting to $8.33 billion in the final quarter of 2024. This weakness is a sign that industry pain might not be over just yet. This weakness from the third quarter to the fourth quarter was driven by strong revenue and the absence of major contract wins. In fact, the company only announced one contract win worth $111 million from the time I wrote about it in October until the end of last year.

Despite these challenges, management does believe that revenue this year will be between $3.85 billion and $4 billion. At the midpoint, that would translate to an increase of 11.4% compared to the $3.52 billion the business reported for 2024. We don't yet know what to expect from a profitability perspective. However, even if we use trading multiples for 2024, shares look quite attractive. In the chart above, you can see where the stock is priced at using figures from last year and the year before. Relative to similar companies, the picture is a bit more complicated. In the table below, I compared Transocean with five such enterprises. On a price to operating cash flow basis, I found that only one of the five companies was cheaper than it is. Another one was tied with it. But when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, four of the five firms were cheaper than our candidate is.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Transocean 4.0 7.8
Seadrill Limited (SDRL) 10.4 3.1
Patterson-UTI Energy (PTEN) 3.0 15.4
Noble Corporation (NE) 6.4 6.3
Helmerich & Payne (HP) 4.0 4.3
Valaris Limited (VAL) 10.0 7.7

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, Transocean is certainly showing some progress. Management seems optimistic about the future, and it is clear that the business has tailwinds working in its favor as opposed to headwinds working against it. Having said that, we do still need to see consistent improvement in the backlog, or at least some indication that the backlog will improve, before this opportunity makes a great deal of sense. Shares are already cheap enough, but industry conditions will be pivotal toward the firm's success. So until we have additional clarity on that front, I believe that maintaining Transocean as a 'hold' is the most appropriate course of action we can take.

data.title: GPTY Vs. AIPI: Choosing The Safer ETF For 30%+ Yields From AI Stocks
data.description:
Off the success of numerous high-yield funds, YieldMax has continued to launch new funds using aggressive options strategies to generate multi-double-digit payouts. One of their most recent launches is the YieldMax AI & Tech Portfolio Option Income ETF (NYSEARCA:GPTY). This fund invests directly in a portfolio of AI and tech stocks selected by the fund managers and uses various options strategies on the portfolio to generate income. Learning about this fund immediately reminded me of the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:AIPI), which I had covered with an initial Buy rating last November. The reason is that AIPI is also a fund that holds a select portfolio of AI-related tech stocks and sells options to generate large 30%+ yields. With GPTY offering similar yields so far but on a weekly distribution, investors may be tempted to switch from AIPI to GPTY or invest in GPTY outright. Further analysis reveals that a more prudent decision, however, would be to invest in AIPI. GPTY's fund managers have far more leeway in their options strategies, some of which introduce risks that make the fund's performance completely unpredictable. This makes GPTY a fund that requires much more historical data than the one-month performance it has to evaluate and not a great buy, considering AIPI exists. I rate AIPI a Buy for investors looking to harvest income from the increasing volatility in AI and tech stocks. GPTY, on the other hand, gets a Hold rating due to its limited history and "black-box" options strategy.

Why AIPI Is The Safer Choice Currently

Both GPTY and AIPI seek similar objectives of providing current income and capital appreciation from an AI-focused tech portfolio plus options. Based on GPTY's last four distributions and current price, yields have averaged about 36.68%. AIPI, on the other hand, has yielded between 36.07% to 40.06% when annualizing previous monthly distributions compared to its current price. Now, one does need to emphasize that GPTY's distributions are weekly, so it's better to compare its last four distributions to AIPI's last distribution with how options pricing changes as market conditions shift. This would be a comparison of 36.68% to 36.07%, suggesting that we would see overall similar distributions between the two funds. Other surface-level comparisons include total returns, where we'll see AIPI outperform GPTY in the short time it's been around. AIPI also offers a smaller management fee of 0.65% compared to GPTY's 0.99%.

This difference in performance can largely be attributed to the different equity holdings in AIPI and GPTY. AIPI consists of 25 stocks, 15 of which overlap with GPTY's 20 stock holdings.

To gain a general picture of why AIPI outperformed GPTY, I took the equity holdings from each fund and backtested the portfolios in Seeking Alpha, creating a scenario where I hypothetically buy each of the holdings in their respective share counts on January 20, 2025, when GPTY launched. What we'll find is that GPTY's equity holdings are up 1.12% overall, while AIPI's holdings are up 2.13%. The two stocks contributing the most to the difference in performance would be SMCI and TSLA. TSLA during this time period was the worst performer having lost about 3.96%, while SMCI was the best performer with a gain of 12.23%. GPTY has a 3.81% position in TSLA and no position in SMCI, while AIPI holds 3.84% in SMCI and has no position in TSLA.

Of course, we cannot bet on this outperformance happening consistently simply by looking at AIPI and GPTY's differences in holdings. You should also avoid choosing one fund over the other because it has stocks you're more bullish on or vice versa. This is because both AIPI and GPTY's stock selection methodologies are vague and mostly unpredictable. Per GPTY's prospectus:

The Fund's investment sub-adviser, ZEGA Financial, LLC ("ZEGA" or the "Sub-Adviser"), selects the AI and Technology Companies in which the Fund will invest.

The other guidelines are very vague and tell us little about how GPTY's equity strategy works. To them, an AI company involves anything from AI software, cloud infrastructure, AI semiconductors, network infrastructure, AI consulting services, software and IT services, technology hardware manufacturing and distribution, semiconductor production, interactive media, and diversified consumer services related to technology and entertainment. It doesn't help that the fund managers also have the capability of investing in foreign companies, which makes it even more difficult to predict what stocks will be in GPTY's portfolio. The only concrete guidelines are that the portfolio will consist of 15–30 stocks and that the companies do need to be liquid enough and have an active options market. But factors like weighting and valuation methodologies have not been disclosed.

AIPI, while slightly better, tells us the same story. The fund tracks an index called the BITA AI Leaders Select Index, which is good because it gives us a backtest going back to 2019.

The methodology also gives us much more to work with than GPTY. Per AIPI's prospectus:

Based on their degree of thematic purity, securities are admitted into the Eligible Universe and assigned to one of two categories: (1) "Purity Leaders," which are companies with a high relative revenue exposure to AI products, services, and activities; and (2) "Key Enablers," which are companies that play a crucial role in facilitating the business application of AI technology, even though their primary business activities may not be solely focused on AI products or services. Securities in the Eligible Universe are ranked and selected as follow within each category:

A. Purity Leaders: Securities are ranked based on their trading volumes and the top 5 are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are weighted by average daily value traded over the prior 12-months,subject to a 10% cap.

B. Key Enablers: Securities are ranked based on their market capitalization and the top 20 securities are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are equally weighted.

The final Index is calculated by aggregating the "Purity Leaders" and "Key Enablers" categories and weighting them in a fixed proportion of 40% and60% respectively.

The Index is rebalanced monthly with Purity Leaders weighted by average12-month trading volume, subject to a 10% cap, and Key Enablers equally weighted. The Index is reconstituted quarterly by employing the process described above.

Unlike GPTY, AIPI's stock selection methodology has a transparent weighting system, and it's clear that no stock will occupy over 10% of the portfolio. However, the issue regarding what particular stock will occupy AIPI's portfolio still remains as what constitutes a "Purity Leader" versus a "Key Enabler" has not been discussed in depth.

GPTY's Options Strategy Involves More Than Just Covered Calls

With GPTY and AIPI's stock selection methodologies providing little indication of how their equity holdings might specifically look like, we must turn to their options strategies to see what fund would make more sense to buy. GPTY's options strategy has me considering a Sell rating for the fund. Its option strategy involves multiple different strategies and not just covered calls. Some of these strategies have been highlighted in the fund's prospectus:

  • covered calls
  • credit spreads
  • diagonal call spread
  • cash-secured put selling
  • calendar call spreads
  • collars

The most important consideration is the cash-secured puts. While all the other strategies are "bearish" plays where you don't want the underlying asset to drastically rise in price due to the sold call option capping your upside, the same cannot be said for cash-secured puts. Selling a cash-secured put is an option strategy where you essentially sell protection for if the underlying asset drops in price. For example, you can sell a cash-secured put on QQQ with a strike price of 450. If QQQ drops below the 450 strike price before the expiration date, you are obligated to buy shares of QQQ for 450 even if the market price is lower. In other words, your ideal case is that QQQ rises in price, so the option expires worthless, and you keep all the premium. GPTY's problem is that the use of both put and call option strategies makes the fund's performance even more unpredictable, especially given the active management of the fund's options. Typically, with a covered call fund, we can generally expect distributions to rise during more volatile market conditions and hence, can make decisions accordingly. The availability of put options in GPTY's strategy, however, means that it can be in a lot more trouble during a downturn if the fund had sold undesirable put options.

The 450 cash-secured put example on QQQ wasn't just a hypothetical example, either. GPTY had sold 381 put contracts on QQQ with a strike price of 450 and an expiration date of March 21, 2025. Fortunately, they are protected with another bought put option on QQQ with same expiration date but a higher strike price of 500. This, however, is an overall losing trade. The option premiums from the 450 QQQ put option do not cover the cost to buy the put option at 500.

GPTY will only profit from this trade if QQQ were to crash and drop below 500, but the real question we should be asking is, "Why is GPTY trading QQQ options?" The implication of this trade is that GPTY has even more flexibility and can trade options on assets not tied to their underlying holdings. With too much flexibility, it is difficult to come up with a buy thesis for GPTY other than you trust and believe in the fund manager.

It's a different story with AIPI which only writes monthly, out-of-the-money ("OTM") covered calls on the individual holdings in its portfolio.

The example you see above with ADBE can be seen across all of AIPI's holdings with different percentages OTM. AIPI will also typically sell calls covering 100% of its portfolio.

Because AIPI uses a "simple" covered call strategy, it is much easier to predict how the fund might perform in certain market conditions, and thus, give you more specific use cases for the fund. The risk with covered call strategies is they cap your upside. We can see this in AIPI compared to its underlying index in which the BITA AI Leaders Select Index has had a total return of approximately 41% since AIPI's inception on June 3, 2024. AIPI, unfortunately, has only seen a total return of 12.86%.

What I want to highlight, however, is how AIPI performed relative to its underlying index when AI stocks underwent a short correction between July 1 and August 31, 2024. AIPI's underlying index saw a drop of approximately 6.09%, while AIPI was able to harvest the volatility using covered calls and reduce the loss to 4.41%.

This kind of predictability means that you can make investment decisions with your input on market conditions. I continue to rate AIPI a Buy because AI stocks have been under pressure lately due to news such as China's DeepSeek. The extra volatility helps AIPI maintain steady payouts and is a great buy if you believe that AI-related stocks have reached a point where their previous growth is no longer sustainable. Of course, if you believe the opposite, then AIPI would not be a buy due to the capped upsides. Whereas with GPTY, its options strategies that include both call and put options on top of assets that are not necessarily tied to its underlying holdings add additional unpredictability to the fund's performance. That said, this unpredictability could be beneficial if the fund's active management pays off. One month's performance, however, is not nearly enough time to see how well the fund managers work in response to various market conditions, and I would not take the risk of buying into GPTY, especially when AIPI exists. I rate GPTY a Hold.

data.title: How To Find The Best Style ETFs For Q1 2025
data.description:
With an ever-growing list of similar-sounding ETFs to choose from, finding the best is an increasingly difficult task. How can investors change the game to shift the odds in their favor?

Don’t Trust Style ETF Labels

There are at least 232 different All Cap Blend ETFs and at least 916 ETFs across twelve styles. Do investors need 76+ choices on average per style? How different can the ETFs be?

Those 232 All Cap Blend ETFs are very different from each other. With anywhere from 1 to 9,729 holdings, many of these All Cap Blend ETFs have drastically different portfolios with differing risk profiles and performance outlooks.

The same is true for the ETFs in any other style, as each offers a very different mix of good and bad stocks. All Cap Value ranks first for stock selection. Small Cap Growth ranks last.

Avoiding Analysis Paralysis

We think the large number of style ETFs hurts investors more than it helps. Manually conducting a deep analysis for every ETF is simply not a realistic option, exposing investors to insufficient analysis and missing profitable opportunities. Analyzing ETFs, with the proper diligence, is far more difficult than analyzing stocks because it means analyzing all the stocks within each ETF. As stated above, there can be as many as 9,729 stocks or more for one ETF.

Figure 1 shows our top-rated ETF for each style.

Figure 1: The Best ETF in Each Style

* Best ETFs exclude ETFs with TNAs less than $100 million for inadequate liquidity

Sources: New Constructs, LLC and company filings

Amongst the ETFs in Figure 1, EA Series Euclidean Fundamental Value ETF (ECML) ranks first overall, Direxion Daily Homebuilders & Supplies Bull 3X Shares ETF (NAIL) ranks second, and ETC 6 Meridian Mega Cap Equity ETF (SIXA) ranks third. iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG) ranks last.

How to Avoid “The Danger Within”

Why do you need to know the holdings of ETFs before you buy?

You need to be sure you do not buy an ETF that might blow up. Buying an ETF without analyzing its holdings is like buying a stock without analyzing its business and finances. No matter how cheap, if it holds bad stocks, the ETF’s performance will be bad.

PERFORMANCE OF FUND’S HOLDINGS – FEES = PERFORMANCE OF FUND

If Only Investors Could Find Style ETFs Rated by Their Holdings

EA Series Euclidean Fundamental Value ETF (ECML) is not only the top-rated All Cap Value ETF but is also the overall top-ranked style ETF out of the 916 style ETFs that we cover.

The worst ETF in Figure 1 is iShares Morningstar Mid-Cap Growth ETF (IMCG), which gets an Attractive rating.

This article was originally published on January 28, 2025.

Disclosure: Hakan Salt owns QVAL. David Trainer, Kyle Guske II, and Hakan Salt receive no compensation to write about any specific stock, sector or theme.
